title: 14_unseen_category_crash.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Score the Fleet Through a Fuel Transition

The underwriting platform retrains a claim-risk model every night and immediately scores the full insured fleet. The pipeline is deliberately boring: one-hot encode `vehicle_type`, fit a gradient-boosted regressor on the training extract, transform tonight's scoring batch with the fitted encoder, write `scored_batch.csv` for the pricing service.

It ran clean for months. Then a logistics customer onboarded three hydrogen trucks, and the nightly job now dies before a single row is scored — the pricing service is stuck serving yesterday's numbers fleet-wide. The training extract has not changed. The model has not changed. Something in the encode step refuses to accept that the world now contains more kinds of vehicles than the training data did. Make the pipeline survive novelty — and make sure you can see how much novelty it is absorbing.
initialCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder

train = pd.read_csv("fleet_train.csv")
batch = pd.read_csv("scoring_batch.csv")

encoder = OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False)
X_train = encoder.fit_transform(train[["vehicle_type"]])
print("fitted categories:", list(encoder.categories_[0]))

model = GradientBoostingRegressor(random_state=0)
model.fit(X_train, train["claim_risk"])

X_batch = encoder.transform(batch[["vehicle_type"]])
scores = model.predict(X_batch)
batch["risk_score"] = scores
batch.to_csv("scored_batch.csv", index=False)
print(f"scored {len(batch)} vehicles")

options.label: Refit the encoder on the scoring batch so it learns the new category
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder

train = pd.read_csv("fleet_train.csv")
batch = pd.read_csv("scoring_batch.csv")

encoder = OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False)
X_train = encoder.fit_transform(train[["vehicle_type"]])
print("fitted categories:", list(encoder.categories_[0]))

model = GradientBoostingRegressor(random_state=0)
model.fit(X_train, train["claim_risk"])

X_batch = encoder.fit_transform(batch[["vehicle_type"]])
scores = model.predict(X_batch)
batch["risk_score"] = scores
batch.to_csv("scored_batch.csv", index=False)
print(f"scored {len(batch)} vehicles")

ValueError: X has 5 features, but GradientBoostingRegressor is expecting 4 features as input.
options.rationale: Refitting on the batch trades one contract violation for another. Tonight's batch has five categories, so the encoder emits five columns into a model trained on four — `predict` rejects the width on the spot. The scarier case is a night when the batch happens to contain exactly four types: the matrix width matches, the alphabetical column order silently reassigns ('hydrogen' takes the slot 'hybrid' trained in), and every prediction is confidently wrong. The fitted vocabulary is part of the model artifact, frozen at training time like the weights themselves.

options.label: Construct the encoder with handle_unknown='ignore' and log the unseen-category rate before scoring
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder

train = pd.read_csv("fleet_train.csv")
batch = pd.read_csv("scoring_batch.csv")

encoder = OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False, handle_unknown="ignore")
X_train = encoder.fit_transform(train[["vehicle_type"]])
print("fitted categories:", list(encoder.categories_[0]))

model = GradientBoostingRegressor(random_state=0)
model.fit(X_train, train["claim_risk"])

unseen = ~batch["vehicle_type"].isin(encoder.categories_[0])
print(f"unseen-rate: {unseen.sum()}/{len(batch)} rows ({unseen.mean():.2%}) -> {sorted(batch.loc[unseen, 'vehicle_type'].unique())}")

X_batch = encoder.transform(batch[["vehicle_type"]])
scores = model.predict(X_batch)
batch["risk_score"] = scores
batch.to_csv("scored_batch.csv", index=False)
print(f"scored {len(batch)} vehicles")
options.resultLog:
fitted categories: ['diesel', 'electric', 'gasoline', 'hybrid']
unseen-rate: 3/1842 rows (0.16%) -> ['hydrogen']
scored 1842 vehicles
options.rationale: `handle_unknown='ignore'` makes transform total instead of partial: a hydrogen row encodes as all zeros across the four learned columns, the model predicts from its 'none of the known types' path, and one novel vehicle can no longer veto scoring for 1,842. The unseen-rate line is the other half of the fix — all-zeros is graceful degradation, not knowledge, so you measure how often it happens, watch the trend, and retrain with 'hydrogen' as a first-class category once it stops being rare. Tolerate novelty at transform time; eliminate it at the next fit.

options.label: Filter the scoring batch down to vehicle types the encoder was fitted on
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder

train = pd.read_csv("fleet_train.csv")
batch = pd.read_csv("scoring_batch.csv")

encoder = OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False)
X_train = encoder.fit_transform(train[["vehicle_type"]])
print("fitted categories:", list(encoder.categories_[0]))

model = GradientBoostingRegressor(random_state=0)
model.fit(X_train, train["claim_risk"])

batch = batch[batch["vehicle_type"].isin(encoder.categories_[0])].copy()
X_batch = encoder.transform(batch[["vehicle_type"]])
scores = model.predict(X_batch)
batch["risk_score"] = scores
batch.to_csv("scored_batch.csv", index=False)
print(f"scored {len(batch)} vehicles")
options.rationale: It runs green, which is exactly the trap: 1839 of 1842 vehicles scored, and the three hydrogen trucks simply vanish from `scored_batch.csv`. The pricing service never hears about them — uninsurable blind spots that no dashboard flags, and the gap grows as the fleet transitions toward the very fuel type being dropped. A pipeline that deletes the inputs it cannot handle has not been fixed; it has learned to hide its failures. If you must exclude rows, that exclusion needs to be loud, counted, and routed somewhere a human will see it.

hints: A fitted encoder's category vocabulary is frozen the moment you call fit, exactly like model weights. The real question is policy: what should transform do when production hands it a value that vocabulary has never contained?

Line 8: `OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False)` leaves every other parameter at its default — and the default policy for categories unseen at fit time is to raise at transform time. The constructor takes an explicit parameter for that policy.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`fit_transform` on line 9 freezes the encoder's vocabulary to the four propulsion types in the training extract. `OneHotEncoder` defaults to `handle_unknown='error'`, so when line 15 transforms a batch containing 'hydrogen', sklearn has no column for it and rejects the entire matrix: `ValueError: Found unknown categories ['hydrogen'] in column 0 during transform`. The asymmetry is what makes this a production classic: train and test splits are drawn from the same historical snapshot, so they share a vocabulary and the bug is unreachable in CI. It can only fire when the input distribution grows — which the real world does on the night nobody is watching.

### Why the fix is correct
`handle_unknown='ignore'` changes transform from partial to total: rows outside the fitted vocabulary encode as all zeros across the learned columns, the model still produces a (conservative) prediction, and scoring completes for the whole fleet. The unseen-rate log is what turns graceful degradation into an operable system — all-zeros is a placeholder, not an answer, so you track its frequency and schedule a retrain that promotes 'hydrogen' to a real column once it matters. The principle: serving-time encoders must be total functions over production inputs, and every fallback they take must be observable.
recruiterReview: Strong choice, and the reasoning matters as much as the parameter: you treated the encoder's vocabulary as part of the frozen model artifact and asked what the transform-time policy for novelty should be — instead of refitting (option A, schema drift straight into the model) or filtering (option C, silently unpriced vehicles, which in this business is the worst outcome on the board). The unseen-rate line is the senior touch; handle_unknown='ignore' without monitoring is how all-zeros rows pile up unnoticed for a quarter. Next iteration: bind encoder and model into one Pipeline artifact, and emit that unseen rate as a metric with an alert threshold rather than a print. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## The Encoder's Vocabulary Is Part of the Model

Every categorical encoder that learns its categories from data — one-hot, ordinal, target — ships with a hidden contract: the set of values it saw during `fit` is frozen into the artifact, exactly like model weights. Training pipelines almost never test that contract, because train/test splits come from the same historical snapshot and therefore share a vocabulary. Production is the first environment where the input distribution is allowed to *grow* — a new product code, a new country, a new fuel type — which is why unseen-category crashes are overwhelmingly discovered at 3 a.m. by a batch job rather than by CI.

### Why 'error' is the default — and when to keep it
sklearn's `OneHotEncoder` defaults to `handle_unknown='error'` deliberately: an unknown category is genuine evidence that the world has drifted from the training distribution, and silently absorbing it is not obviously safe. Keeping the default is right when an unknown value means data corruption — a typo'd enum, a broken upstream join — and a hard stop is cheaper than a wrong prediction. The mistake is not the default itself; it is shipping the default to a scoring path where one novel value can veto thousands of valid rows.

### The graceful-degradation policies
- `handle_unknown='ignore'` — unseen values encode as all zeros across the learned columns. The model sees "none of the known categories" and predicts from its fallback path. Scoring survives; information about the new category is lost until retrain.
- `handle_unknown='infrequent_if_exist'` with `min_frequency` — groups rare *and* unseen categories into a shared infrequent bucket, so novelty maps to "rare" rather than to "nothing". Often the better behavioral match.
- For ordinal and target encoders, the analogue is `unknown_value` or an explicit global prior — same principle, different parameter.

Whichever policy you choose, choose it at fit time, inside the artifact — not with try/except wrappers bolted around transform in the serving code.

### Degradation without observation is decay
All-zeros encoding is first aid, not treatment. If 0.1% of rows are unseen tonight and 9% are unseen next quarter, the model is increasingly predicting from its fallback path and nobody decided that. The other half of any unseen-category fix is a measurement: compute the unseen rate per batch (a membership test against `encoder.categories_`), log it next to the scoring count, and alert past a threshold. The alert's playbook is a retrain that promotes the new category to a real column. Tolerate novelty at transform; eliminate it at the next fit.

### Common variations of this bug
- `LabelEncoder` misused on features, crashing with "y contains previously unseen labels" the first time a new value reaches serving.
- Target encoding that maps unseen categories to NaN, which then crashes — or silently zeroes — the downstream model instead of falling back to the global mean.
- `pd.get_dummies` pipelines producing different column sets for train and serve, silently misaligning features instead of erroring.
- Embedding lookup tables in deep models raising index errors when a categorical id exceeds the vocabulary built at training time.
- Feature-store joins returning NULL for newly onboarded entities, breaking imputers that were fitted before those entities existed.
